You might possibly die.'"   [04:48]  "That's like pretty much what I say going into every engagement just because of the expectations of PE that people have with sales teams. And it really comes down to a lack of understanding."   [05:02]  "I'm not a magic pill solution. We still have the same problems that everybody else has. We're just really good at solving them. And that's sol that's sales management. You shouldn't try to like aim to have a problemless organization. you need to spend a ton of time training and creating uh transferable processes that just make you really efficient and really consistent in how you solve them." [05:48]  "And so, we're going to go pretty deep. It's monotonous. It's adult babysitting, managing sales reps. Most of your time is uh spent reinforcing the basics. And there's really no finish line, right? You just do the same thing over and over and over and over again."   [06:08]  "Now, I say this as a joke, but it's not one. [06:30]  "Like the amount of things that I have seen happen that takes a best like top performer to your literal literal worst performer is insane. It's outrageous."   [06:42]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Lower Thirds Management Focus"   NÚMEROS:  Não especificados   VISUAL:  Conceito de focar nos performers mais baixos, não nos top   [06:45]  "And so what you have to get really really good at it's your sales management. So, what you want to get really good at is how you can evaluate, manage, and develop the underperforming reps. Right? There's a concept we talk about called lower thirds."   [07:02]  "If you are on the top of the leaderboard, and I mean this almost to a literal perspective, we do not talk to you. We don't want to say anything. We don't want to give advice. Like, I have ripped my managers apart before by trying to do a call review on a rep that's at the top of the leaderboard. Leave them alone."   [07:22]  "Don't say anything to them except obviously little culture pieces to make them feel important, appreciated, acknowledged, etc. Right? It's all about spending your time with the rest of the team to develop them into in and beyond KPI. Right? It's the most leveraged activity in the company."   [07:42]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "The Core Four"   NÚMEROS:  4 componentes principais   VISUAL:  Lista dos quatro pilares principais da gestão de vendas   [07:45]  "So, we have something as we start to get into this, we have something called the core four, right? Number two is pipeline management. Number three is performance management. And number four is training center optimization because you always want to create leverage. You always want to create transferable value, right?"   [08:20]  "So they are always focused on uploading like up-to-date fresh material in the training center. [12:00]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Leadership & Ramp Up"   NÚMEROS:  Não especificados   VISUAL:  Nova seção iniciando com onboarding e liderança   [12:05]  "Now, what seemed the most intuitive to me to to kind of segue into this is I'm gonna start with leadership and ramp up that seemed the most sense, you know, we're almost going to take it from like think of consumer journey, but instead of like a consumer, it's like the rep's journey almost and we'll kind of follow that all the way through and what we do at each every single step of the way."   [12:30]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "First Principles - Influence-Based Leadership"   NÚMEROS:  Não especificados   VISUAL:  Citações de John Maxwell sobre liderança   [12:35]  "So, we'll start with leadership and ramp up. Um, all right. So, some first principles to our leadership approach and style. And I'm going to start with a few quotes, right? I have an entire training called influence-based leadership rather than, you know, I guess the alternative authority based leadership."   [12:55]  "So, here's a couple examples. Leadership is not about titles, positions, or flowcharts. It's about one life influencing another. John Maxwell, right? One of the most popular leadership gurus."   [13:10]  "Second one, also John Maxwell. People buy into the leader before they buy into the vision. Right? Third one from our chief sales officer. Nobody cares how much you know until they know how much you care."   [13:28]  "Right? The the point of these quotes is that your sales management, sales leadership, and this I mean a fish rots from the head down. So this goes all the way into your executive team or if you're the owner or the CEO, it starts with you. But we have to lead with influence."   [13:48]  "And one of the number one things that I see with sales managers, if we get brought into a deal or there's some internal sales manager on there, it's very bizarre to me. I'm always so thrown off. It's this like very forced like you better fuck close or you're out of here type of style. And it's the most ineffective thing you could possibly imagine, right?"   [14:15]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Sales Principles Applied to Management"   NÚMEROS:  Não especificados   VISUAL:  Paralelos entre vendas e gestão de equipe   [14:20]  "What we have to remember if if assuming that you hired the right person is that everybody's in sales management. They're in high in a highintensity sales operation because we've had a high level of sales success ourselves."   [14:38]  "Now, let me say this. A great sales rep does not make you a great sales manager. But guys, I'll tell you, it's really, really hard to find a great sales manager that wasn't a great sales rep at a certain point in time."   [14:55]  "And it's also really hard to get buy in if you can't jump on the phone and in the trenches yourself to show somebody how it's done, right?"   [15:08]  "So, if you're a great salesperson and now you're a sales leader, a sales executive, we can't forget the sales and influence-based principles that got us here just because we're given a title and a little bit of authority, right? So, we call this influence-based leadership rather than authoritybased leadership."   [15:30]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Sales Principles = Leadership Principles"   NÚMEROS:  Não especificados   VISUAL:  Lista comparativa de princípios de vendas aplicados à liderança   [15:35]  "So, let's talk about some sales principles, right? Imagine you're on a sales call. Just to make some parallels here, you don't tell somebody what to do when you're on a sales call, right? You Well, maybe Jeremy does, right? But most of the time, you guide them to the solution, right?"   [15:55]  "You you do uh you need a strong discovery and you have to listen before you can be confident in the solution, right? You want to seek to understand. Sales is about getting people more of what they want in life, right? We call it core desires and DBMs, which stands for dominant buying motives."   [16:18]  "Right? When I'm saying these things that are true about sales, I want you to be applying them to sales management, sales leadership. Right? Objection handling starts with listening and diffusing, not arguing and meeting head-on with resistance, right?"   [16:38]  "Are you guys seeing the parallels? When you have a SA sales manager, they should be seeking to understand. They should be collecting the evidence. They should start to figure out what motivates a rep on an individual very personal level, right? To figure out how do I position these processes in a way that shows them it's going to help them hit their level of income."   [17:05]  "Okay? We're going to start very basic. These are basic concepts. That's why they're called first principles, and we're going to build on top of it. But I'm telling you, even these things right here, most people from what I see aren't doing them."   [17:22]  "If you can't build trust, you can't make a sale. Right? If you can't build trust, you cannot lead a team because they don't trust you because you don't have credibility because they don't think that you could actually do it."   [17:38]  "The more you can demonstrate expertise, the higher level of confidence they have that your solution is right for them, right? Lead by example and they buy into you before they buy into the product, right? It's it's the same with leadership."   [17:55]  "Really effective leaders, they lead with their vision, right? And so, you know, if you're a sales manager, actually, let me ask you this real quick. Who has like an info offer? Education offer. All right, keep your hands up for a second."   [18:15]  "Now, if you don't have a info offer, raise your fuck hand. Thank you. All right. Are you or a marketer for an info offer? Thank you. All right. If you don't have an info offer, raise your hand if you have a sales team for whatever else."   [18:35]  "So, like pretty much everyone's hand at that that point is up, right? All right. So, if you if you either have a salesman, you can put down now. So, you either have a sales manager or you are the sales manager."   [18:50]  "And so, everybody wants to start with this influence-based stuff and and just show them how much you care and that you could get them exactly what you want, right? We do vision exercises, right?"   [19:05]  "And there's also a leadership quote that says like if somebody can't fit their vision into yours, they're going to leave at some point. Like your vision has to be big enough. Your company vision has to be big enough where everyone inside it can can fit in there or they outgrow it, right?"   [19:28]  "It's just you have to look at your sales team the same way, right? If they want to make a certain amount of income, you have to show them what that would look like in terms of process, in terms of KPIs, in terms of strategy."   [19:45]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Hiring Process - Highly Competitive Interview"   NÚMEROS:  Não especificados   VISUAL:  Estrutura do processo de contratação   [19:50]  "All right, so let's start at the beginning. Let's start with hiring them. I'll start breaking down all of our different processes. What I thought would make the most sense, I'm going to shed light on all of these different things."   [20:05]  "So, it all starts with a highly competitive interview process. Right now, our sales directors do not do this to be clear, but this is where it all starts. This is our talent acquisition team. I have an amazing uh director of talent acquisition. She's very good."   [20:25]  "We have a extremely in-depth interview process. It's very high friction, right? I'm going to try to just sprinkle nuggets on all of these different things as I go throughout it. A lot of them aren't on the slide. They'll just come to mind for me."   [20:42]  "One thing is this. People are always very afraid. They're nervous to uh to put a lot of friction in their in their hiring funnel. Let me start over. Most people don't have a hiring funnel."   [20:58]  "If you do have a hiring funnel or process, they're afraid to add too much friction. We treat our talent acquisition like most people treat customer acquisition, right? Lots of funnels, lots of ad spin, lots of platforms."   [21:15]  "We literally have full-on VSSLs, full-on like sales pages, we would call them, testimonials and success and case studies from our different uh from people in our sales team. We treat it like customer acquisition. It's the same exact process, right?"   [21:35]  "And the only difference I would say, and I'm not I'm not saying it necessarily always is the opposite with customer acquisition, but in talent acquisition, your A player, something really interesting happens, especially in sales."   [21:52]  "You want your best sales people are always going to be very competitive. They have these in intrinsic drivers. So the more competitive and the more friction there is to an extent obviously everything has to be within reason. The more the top performers want that position, right?"   [22:15]  "It's it's it's a little bit different than customer acquisition where your highest quality leads like sometimes you don't want to add too much friction. It's almost the opposite here. Our best talent wants to position that much more because they're like, 'Oh fuck these guys don't mess around.' like that's the role that I want. Right?"   [22:38]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "High Friction Process Steps"   NÚMEROS:  Múltiplas etapas de entrevista   VISUAL:  Fluxograma do processo de contratação com múltiplos pontos de avaliação   [22:42]  "So, it's a very high friction process. Within that process, they have to go through a skills assessment. So, there's a lot of interviews. They go through, they fill out the initial submission, which like I said, it's like a type form application because it's all in funnels."   [23:00]  "Uh we request a submission video, then they have an interview, then they have a second interview, then they get put into a skills assessment."   [23:10]  "Now, right here is where the sales director steps in. Now, our talent acquisition team used to do all the skills assessments because there's nothing really to it. It's a mock call."   [23:25]  "So, you would pretend that you're a lead, that you're a prospect, and then you would, you know, conduct the call. It's a skills assessment. But the reason a long time ago now, we had our our sales management team do the skills assessments is because you want them to have ownership from day one, right?"   [23:48]  "Like what would happen is they'd be like, 'Ah, yeah, he sucks, but like I wouldn't have hired him if it was up to me.' And I was like, 'All right, it's now up to you. Don't let it happen again.' Right?"   [24:02]  "So, it starts with this extreme level of ownership where we want them to take full ownership of their roster, right? And they have to sell us, by the way, on why they think that candidate should be placed on their team, right?"   [24:20]  "And there's a there's a quantitative scoring process that we leverage AI for as well, by the way. So, anytime they score under a certain number on their skills assessment, they have to give us a really good case for why they want to hire them for their team."   [24:38]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Standardized Skills Assessment"   NÚMEROS:  3 horas de conteúdo de preparação   VISUAL:  Estrutura de treinamento pré-avaliação e sistema de pontuação   [24:42]  "All right. Now, in our skills assessment, everything is standardized because you have to compare apples to apples. The biggest I've I've had I've seen companies try to do skills assessments and the biggest mistake that I see with it is like they'll say I've seen some weird stuff."   [25:05]  "They'll get on a call and they'll say, 'Okay, just like pitch me your last product and I'll be the lead.' And it's like, first of all, it's going to come down to how well they knew their last product, which isn't really the point here, is it?"   [25:22]  "Are you just trying to see how well they did with that last offer? Or are you trying to see how well they would do in your environment? And are you trying to see how well they can take instruction and direction? And are you trying to see how trainable and coachable they are?"   [25:40]  "See, these are all the things that we want to evaluate in a new candidate. So, what we do is everybody gets access to a training center. That's our skills assessment training center. They have to consume about 3 hours of content just to prepare for the skills assessment."   [26:00]  "And we have little I don't know what you'd call it, little tricks within there where it says, 'Hey, by the way, if you're watching the video, that tells us that you got this far. When you're in the skills assessment, make sure you say X.' And we do that and it's part of the score so that we know they actually consumed everything, right?"   [26:22]  "And we know they're paying attention and we could see how fast their processes are because the speed of implementation. So everything is standardized. So we compare apples to apples."   [26:35]  "Now in that standardization we have a skills assessment document that our skills directors use. They ask the same exact questions every time. They have the same obs exact objections every time. They come as and show up as the same avatar, right? Because you have to remove any of the subjectivity."   [26:58]  "What if this person scores high on a skills assessment because this person had harder objections? So we remove all subjectivity from this. Right?"   [27:08]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Onboarding Process - A Player Environment"   NÚMEROS:  Não especificados   VISUAL:  Screenshots de documentação de processo de onboarding   [27:12]  "Next step is when you actually onboard the sales team. When you hire an A player, the first thing that you need them to know is that they're in an A player environment. The confidence starts from from day one, right?"   [27:28]  "So, our onboarding process is immaculate. We we treat it so seriously like it's freaking customer fulfillment, right? We have an entire these are just screenshots of some of our process documentation."   [27:45]  "I'm going to do that the whole time because there's a lot of people that talk in the space. So, I'm a big fan of showing. I'm just going to have screenshots of a lot of stuff throughout this."   [28:00]  "Uh, we have a a very detailed onboarding SOP that obviously the talent acquisition team uses and then we have their training guide and then we have a checklist. You can see the one that I used here is for the we we do this for every position by the way."   [28:20]  "So, sometimes I'll give an example of a closer, sometimes there'll be a setter, sometimes sales manager. We do the same thing for all of them. Um, but there's different screenshots here and they have a checklist of how they go throughout it."   [28:38]  "All right. With me so far?" "Yeah." "All right. Awesome. So you guys are like, 'You got two iPads still. I forgot about those.'"   [28:50]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Initial Ramp Up Training - Stripped Down Essentials"   NÚMEROS:  14 dias de conteúdo reduzido para 5 dias   VISUAL:  Comparação antes/depois do processo de treinamento   [28:55]  "All right. Um, so then, so then let's talk about initial ramp up training. Now, as I go throughout here, I'm going to try really hard, and I was thinking as I was building this presentation, I'm going to try really hard to think of like the paradigm shifts or the big needle movers that I've learned over the years. And uh, this is actually one of them."   [29:20]  "So obviously anyone with half a brain knows that you don't just onboard a sales rep with no training. What a lot of people don't realize is how intentional you are with your training makes a massive difference."   [29:38]  "So we went so far because over the years we just kept adding and kept adding and kept adding to our training center that we would have a rep start and they were in like 14 days worth of content for like eight hours a day before they hit the phones. And yeah, it was crazy."   [30:00]  "And basically, nobody remembered what they went through by the time it was over. It was just so much information, information overload. Not to mention in sales, it's a very kinetic learning motion. You don't really learn in sales just by studying. You learn by doing."   [30:20]  "And so most of the ramp up actually happens once they hit the phones. Make sense? Most of you guys might know that. So what we do is we actually went the opposite way with our training and onboarding process and we said how do we strip out literally everything that is non-essential for ramping only make them go through the most essential things to start with"   [30:45]  "and then once they're on phones we've actually gified it and they have to unlock certain levels of training right like literally like no you can't watch that yet you're not good enough right once you get the basics down once you've proven us that you could implement the most basic stuff."   [31:05]  "Now, we're going to let you go through this training that you know most people would charge a lot of money. Now, I'll tell you guys this. I'm not even going to use names. There's some big trainers in the space, sales trainers. Everybody, every one of you just a name came to your mind. Okay?"   [31:25]  "That person, we have reps all the time that come into our organization and they say, 'I paid 30 grand for that training program and it didn't even scratch the surface on the training that we get from you guys.' Right?"   [31:40]  "And here's my point with saying that. I'm not saying, 'Look how good we are.' My point in saying that is we were completely undervaluing and watering down how valuable our stuff was."   [31:55]  "And it put us in this defense mode where we were like trying to make people go through training and we're like, 'Why didn't you watch the training today? You were supposed to watch.' And we're like babysitting all the time."   [32:10]  "And so the psychology is so interesting. We said, 'Bro, you're not ready to look at this. Like go get a few more deals and we'll talk about you going through this level of training.' Right now everybody just can't wait to unlock the training."   [32:28]  "So psychology plays a really big piece, a really big role into game gamifying sales reps. You have to understand how sales reps and I I am a sales rep at heart. So I think the same way, right? I'm like, dude, give me the big fuck But if I have it all right there day one, I'm like, I'll look at it when I can."   [32:50]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "The Big Three"   NÚMEROS:  3 componentes essenciais, 5 dias para phones   VISUAL:  Lista dos três elementos críticos do treinamento inicial   [32:55]  "Okay. All right. So we only have them go through the big three, which I'm going to show you here in a second. It's basically uh discovery, pitch, personalization, and closing sequence."   [33:10]  "The big three to us means that these are the three biggest steps that you can't skip, right? They're all important. If one of them wasn't important, it would be called the nine steps to wires from strangers. They're all important, but you can't miss these three."   [33:28]  "So, we start with the big three. We teach the anatomy of the talk track. So, they memorize the actual talk track. That's our word for script. I don't say script because it's too rigid and clunky. Talk track guides the conversation. It gives them a way to think. It doesn't tell them word for word."   [33:50]  "They go through the essential process training, like the basics of pipeline management and the essential product knowledge training because you have to know what you're selling and that's it. And then they hit the phones."   [34:05]  "And the cool thing about that is they're usually on phones within 5 days of hiring. And we see way better results from that because the training is more fresh in their mind. They implement right away and then like I said, they start unlocking training as they're learning it in real time."   [34:25]  "Now, I didn't put it in this deck, but um and I think I might even talk about it later. In fact, I do, so I won't go too much into it. But, um, no matter how good a sales rep is, no matter how much experience they have, every single rep is going to go through a ramping period."   [34:45]  "Every single one, because every offer is different, every avatar is different, the product knowledge is different, the objections are different. You kind of have to fill it out. So, you have to get a certain amount of calls and reps in before you really start hitting your stride, which is another reason we do it the way that we do it."   [35:08]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Pre-Phone Assessments"   NÚMEROS:  3 tipos de avaliação, 2 chances para passar   VISUAL:  Sistema de três avaliações antes de permitir ligações   [35:12]  "All right. Now before they actually uh hit the sales floor, this is another thing I highly recommend you guys do. We used to just look at consumption, right? Like ah did they complete the training in the training center and we just people just still didn't know what they were doing."   [35:32]  "It's weird. You have to like make it so basic. So they have a skills assessment to get hired like we said. Then to get placed on calls, they have to pass another skills assessment with the director which is specific to the offer they're selling on. Right?"   [35:52]  "So they have they have to they pass that. It's a a mock roleplay. It's all standardized again with the actual talk track and offer they're selling. Then they have to pass a written assessment uh or sorry a written process assessment. The basics on like CRM pipeline management whatever it is."   [36:15]  "And then a written product knowledge assessment. Those are the three areas. They have to pass all three to be placed on phones. You have two times, two chances. We take it that seriously."   [36:30]  "And yes, there's people that have failed and we've fired them because if you can't do something as simple as prepare to like pass an assessment to be on phones, you can't work for us. Somebody will hire you, but we won't."   [36:48]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Evaluating Performance Through Ramping"   NÚMEROS:  60-70% de KPIs estabilizados   VISUAL:  Framework de avaliação durante período de ramping   [36:52]  "All right. So then we go, let's talk about evaluating performance through ramping. So you see how we're just go taking it from the very beginning from hiring and we'll continue on as they as they go through."   [37:10]  "Sounds like I'm talking about reps. actually talking about sales management as it relates to reps obviously because that's who we're managing."   [37:20]  "All right. So, what we look at, this is a big question I get. They say, 'Well, how do you evaluate uh new reps, right? New reps that are in ramping. What do you look for?'"   [37:35]  "Now, I I don't know like why exactly I landed on this number, just a lot of years of data and a lot of reps that we've hired and managed. Our ramping KPIs are 60 to uh 65 to 70% of stabilized, right?"   [37:55]  "So stabilized KPI means what what is the KPIs? What do we want the them to hit once they're stabilized? Once they're actually like in their stride, they've built a pipeline and they're starting to perform. We take it we want to see about 65 to 70% of that when they're ramping in those first few weeks."   [38:20]  "And I'm going to break down an exact ramping cadence in a second on like how long that takes. Um but 65 to 70%. Why? Because if they're at 20%, they're probably never going to get there, right? They're never going to get to stabilize if they are that far off."   [38:40]  "But we know, like I said, that every rep takes time to ramp. So 65 to 70% just has always felt right. And it and it and it works about the same every time."   [38:55]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Pipeline Maturity Based On Sales Cycle"   NÚMEROS:  2 semanas exemplo de sales cycle   VISUAL:  Framework de consideração de maturidade de pipeline   [39:00]  "Now, what you have to consider when you're looking at these ramping KPIs is the pipeline maturity based on the sales cycle. All right, this is an important concept."   [39:15]  "Like I hate the attorney answers. Like we all hate attorneys, right? if you're an attorney here, I'm sorry. I don't really like your kind. And I pay them a lot of money. I I make the joke of, you know, I've put a lot of attorneys kids through college and they just drive me nuts because like the more inefficient they are."   [39:38]  "Anyways, like Jeremy said, this is therapy for us. So, thank you. But the more inefficient they are, like the more money they make. They're not really incentivized in that way."   [39:50]  "But anyways, my point here was I hate attorneys answers where like everything is it depends, you know, but they don't really tell you what it depends on. But some of that relates to this, but I'll give you the framework to think through."   [40:08]  "When somebody says, 'Well, how long do you give them before you fire them?' Well, it depends. And these are the things it depends on. First of all, I look at the ramping KPIs. They should be hitting something, not just goose egging for months on end and you just keep them and hope and cross your fingers, right?"   [40:30]  "So, I look at stabilized KPIs. The second thing is pipeline maturity based on sales cycle. What I mean is if you have a twoe sales cycle, right? Okay. iPad opportunity. If you have a two week sales cycle, when should you see your first sale?"   [40:52]  "All right, we won't give out an iPad yet. Almost everyone got it. No, it's not. It's It's not a trick question. Two weeks. But this is what's so crazy. Every one of you know that, right? It's so simple."   [41:10]  "Yet, we can hire a sales rep on an offer with a two-eek sales cycle and by seven days later, a client can be freaking out that this sales rep hasn't closed a deal yet. It's like the most bizarre thing."   [41:28]  "And as business owners, you guys might do that, too. And people start getting really antsy. Well, what's going on? Why isn't this rep closing anything yet? So, you have to take in consideration."   [41:42]  "I see some fingers pointing. I won't look over there. Um, so you have to take in consideration the sales cycle because that, by the way, that holds true in perpetuity."   [41:55]  "I'll tell you, the longer the sales cycle, the more complex this stuff gets. Um, my fiance, she's a vice president of sales for a very heavily funded SAS company. Their average sales cycle 60 to 90 days. It's really hard because now you have to give that type of sales cycle to measure any type of performance improvements."   [42:20]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Forecasting Reports - Pacing Indicators"   NÚMEROS:  50% probabilidade como threshold   VISUAL:  Sistema de forecasting baseado em probabilidade de fechamento   [42:25]  "Right? Now, do you wait to do anything? No, you have to look at what we call pacing indicators, which brings me to the next point. We use forecasting reports, forecasted reports."   [42:40]  "So, let's say that you have a longer sales cycle. The mistake that people would learn, let's say that they have the first point that I just went over down, and they go, 'Okay, yeah, it's a long sales cycle, so we'll just wait and see.' They look at nothing and they just like weeks go by and then they're like, 'Okay, where are things at?'"   [43:05]  "So we look at our pacing indicators which obviously in marketing you have your leading indicators like cost per lead, cost per call, whatever it is. And then in sales with a extended sales cycle and an offline conversion you have your lagging indicators which would be the basic stuff like sales close rate, revenue, average sales price etc."   [43:30]  "Well, what happens in between those things? You can't just hold your breath. You might go out of business in some cases. So we have pacing indicators and the main ones that you look at is in forecasted reports."   [43:48]  "So, in the CRM, um, and and I think I might have a slide on this later, but again, in the Q&A, we could go a little bit more in depth if you guys care about this. They are forecasting based on deal probability."   [44:05]  "So, anything that has a probability of closing under 50%, they do. It doesn't count. It doesn't go into the forecast report because if it's literally a coin flip, I don't care about that fuck Like, it's more probable to not happen just knowing that everybody overestimates as well."   [44:28]  "So, it's that's what we do. It's forecasted and it's based on a weighted forecast which just helps it be a little bit more accurate. So what we do is we look at the forecasted report building. We compare that to the amount of calls that they're taking and we fast forward in our minds mentally to say in 2 weeks or however long it is, would they fall between that 65 to 70% KPI?"   [44:58]  "You see what we're doing there? if their forecasted report isn't growing at all, you don't even have the opportunity to hit those KPIs. You're just waiting for weeks for nothing to happen, right?"   [45:15]  "So, this is how we analyze and we can kind of like predict the future in a way. And by the way, it's one of the coolest things ever. It doesn't happen all the time. Some of our clients don't ask any questions at all. Like, they're just so far removed. They just like celebrate when a deal comes in."   [45:35]  "But some of them, they'll get very antsy, like I was saying. They get very like where what's happening? Where are deals? What's happening? and they'll freak out. And one of the most satisfying things for me is one of my sales director replies like, 'Dude, relax. We got 150K that's supposed to come good in the next 5 days.' And then it happens."   [45:58]  "There's like nothing cooler, right? They're like, 'Oh my god, you guys are fortune tellers.' And we're like, 'No, it's just a CRM.' But it's awesome when you can get into in in tune with this stuff."   [46:15]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Qualitative Call Analysis - Process Compliance First"   NÚMEROS:  Não especificados   VISUAL:  Framework de análise qualitativa focado em conformidade de processo   [46:20]  "The next thing you would do is qualitative call analysis. So when somebody's ramping every week on on Monday we have a WPM. It stands for weekly pulse meeting. I have one with my operations team and I have one with my sales management team."   [46:40]  "On the management team, our sales directors, they give us their validation goals and their critical uh critical areas of focus, which is basically like what they're focused on for that week. The validation goal is what on their team are they trying to validate."   [47:00]  "And with new sales reps, they're just trying to validate that, you know, they're headed in the right direction. And so, like I said, everything you ever want to know is on the sales calls. The first thing that we look for, other than the couple of items I just said, is process compliance. That's the the first thing that we care about."   [47:22]  "Are they following the process? And the reason why is because sometimes you could break it down so simply. It's skill verse will, right? Like, I don't even care about the skill sets. Are they willing to do what it takes? Right?"   [47:40]  "Because if they're willing to do what it takes, I could do more with that rep than than someone that's talented that like won't do anything you tell them to do, right? Again, maybe you're a top performer. You just can't work for us. We have a way we do things because that's how we consistently, you know, hit the results we have."   [48:02]  "So, you really want to measure skill verse will and look at their process compliance, which leads me and segus us into the next point, call analysis."   [48:15]  "Uh, how are you liking this so far, guys? Is this good?" "All right. Awesome. Right on."   [48:25]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Call Analysis - Everything You Want To Know Is On The Calls"   NÚMEROS:  Não especificados   VISUAL:  Ênfase repetida na importância da análise de chamadas   [48:30]  "All right. Let's go into call analysis. Everything you want to know is on the calls. And I mean like literally in fact I had we'll call it a dispute for lack of a better term."   [48:45]  "Um but I had like a dispute with the client once and he was like um arguing about like well what are you hearing on calls and blah blah blah. And I'm like dude wait hold on a second. Like why the hell are you not watching calls?"   [49:02]  "This was like the internal marketer. I'm like, when I say everything we want to know on calls, I'm not just referring to sales management. You want to know lead quality, go listen to a call, right? You want to know the objections we're getting, go listen to a call."   [49:20]  "Like marketers, in my opinion, should be listening to calls just as much as sales management, right? And and by the way, like I don't control marketing, so I don't tell them what to do. But if they work for me, I would be telling my marketers to listen to the sales calls because it's the same information they want to know."   [49:42]  "Since the marketers don't work for us, a lot of the times we do that for do it for them. And I'll show you what that feedback loop looks like."   [49:52]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Call Analysis Process - Feedback Broken Into Phases"   NÚMEROS:  3 fases de feedback   VISUAL:  Sistema de feedback estruturado por níveis de performance   [49:58]  "All right. Okay. So, are they on track and are they following process? So, let's talk about our call analysis process. Again, I'm going to break down some of the biggest nuggets that we've learned over the years."   [50:15]  "And it's I like to say this, like it's not the most complicated thing, but it's very, very nuanced. That's the thing with sales, especially when you have a big sales team, cuz it's all about leverage, right? A small bit a small difference makes a big difference."   [50:35]  "In this process, there's a a few nuances that that literally changed everything for us. Like, from not working at all to like, whoa, how did we do that?"   [50:48]  "So the first one is that feedback is broken up into different phases. I'm going to show you that on the next slide. It shows you what the phases are. But what we realize, what most managers do that is the biggest mistake with sales talent is they'll do a call review and they'll be like, 'These are the 50 things you did wrong.'"   [51:10]  "And the sales rep will say something that sounds like, 'Awesome, thank you. I'll work on that.' Which translates to, 'Awesome, thank you. I'll never be able to do the 50 things that you just said and I don't even remember them already. Right?"   [51:28]  "So what you have to do is based on the different phases which again I'll show you but it's basically nonvalidated, underperforming, stabilized and then overperforming. Those are the three different buckets of management tiers. We are giving them very different feedback. Okay?"   [51:50]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Three Management Tiers"   NÚMEROS:  Fase 1, 2, 3   VISUAL:  Tabela mostrando três fases com diferentes tipos de feedback   [51:55]  "So if it's a brand new rep, we are giving them feedback on process compliance, meaning following the talk track in the big three. I don't care how well they handle objections. I don't care if they mess up the pitch. Like, I don't care about any of those things. This is all we're focusing on."   [52:15]  "And the reason why is because if they can't even do the basics, why are we going to waste our time focusing on the advanced things? Okay, so this this will build on itself in just a second."   [52:30]  "Um, and then we emphasize the big three. So, here are the tiers right here. It was I have a whole document on this, but it doesn't fit on a slide, but essentially phase one is nonvalidated, ramping, and underperforming reps."   [52:48]  "So, feedback type, we're focusing on the big three. This is directly from an SOP that our sales directors use, right? We're focusing on the big three in technical compliance."   [53:02]  "Now, technical compliance is the only reason it's specified that way. I'm not talking about FTC compliance. That's a really big part of what we do, but that's not what I'm talking about here. Technical compliance means they're following the technical process, right? They're like I'm going to show you some of those things in a second."   [53:25]  "All right. So, a couple of questions, right? Are they following the talk track in the sales process? And then the big three, like I said, discovery, pitch, personalization, like did they do a strong discovery? Did they do an irresistible pitch? Did they follow a clean closing sequence? Right?"   [53:48]  "It's funny, too, because on that third one, closing sequence, the amount of calls that our team has analyzed from, and I even mean good reps, the amount of calls we see where people just don't ask for the business is like insane."   [54:05]  "Like sales managers over here, I know a couple of you guys, you would agree with me where you're like, 'What the hell?' Right? Like you'll watch a call that was like so good you're like, 'Ah yeah, this going to be a poster call. I'm putting this in the training center and then they just like won't ask for the business and they'll set up a follow-up call.'"   [54:28]  "It's weird how often they need to be reminded of this. But anyways, that's the three things that we focus on if they're in phase one."   [54:38]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Phase Two - Stabilized Reps"   NÚMEROS:  10 steps detalhados   VISUAL:  Detalhamento de feedback para reps estabilizados   [54:42]  "Phase two means it's a stabilized rep, meaning they either are hitting KPI or they should be hitting KPI. Now, we go into detailed feedback on what we call the 10 steps."   [54:58]  "So, for us, it's like warm-up, connectivity, agenda, frame, information, confirmation, strategic transitions, like it's it's all of the nuance stuff that details the rest of our process. In part two of the training today, I'm going to go over what those are. So, just we'll come back to that."   [55:20]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Phase Three - Top Performers"   NÚMEROS:  Não especificados   VISUAL:  Feedback avançado para top performers   [55:25]  "And then phase three is our top performers. That's advanced feedback where we're literally show them like I have a whole presentation on advanced trust building reverse psychology baby negatives like uh strategic qualifiers like it's it's all the psych like psychological Jedi mind tr Jedi mindset what do they call it" "Jedi mind tricks"   [55:48]  "that is what we do on the on the third phase right they have to like earn the privilege to get that type of feedback right does that make sense why I do this" "right"   [56:02]  "Have you guys ever heard of those different phases in management, by the way?" "No. No."   [56:10]  "No. And I was going to challenge you if you said yes because I've never seen this in my life and we had to build this because it wasn't By the way, I want I want you guys to know something. This is not a lack of willingness in most cases. It's a lack of ability."   [56:28]  "Like nobody can take that amount of information and just implement it on the next call. And in most call reviews that I see uh or have seen over the years from sales directors, they're like, they play two minutes, they pause it. Okay, I would have done this differently."   [56:48]  "They p They play two minutes, they pause it. Okay, you should have asked this question. They play two minutes, they pause it. You could have took him deeper there. Oh, you didn't unpack that. By the end of the call, you're like, 'Wait, what did he even say I did wrong?' Right? Or she."   [57:08]  "So, this is really important. Break it down to the stupid, right? Like I I am a big believer of this is simple scales. Don't add complexity to scale. It makes things really complicated."   [57:25]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Both Sides Of The Sales Call"   NÚMEROS:  4 dozen testimonials mencionados   VISUAL:  Framework de análise bilateral das chamadas   [57:30]  "All right. Next thing, both sides of the sales call. So, our sales directors are also trained to listen to both sides of the sales call. Even though marketers should also listen to sales calls, why do we listen to both sides of the sales call?"   [57:48]  "Because it would make absolutely no sense if they're listening to what the rep's saying and then they just ignore everything the lead's saying while they're on the same freaking call. Everything we want to know is on the call."   [58:05]  "So, we have something called a management tracker that every director has for every brand and offer. and what they do. And these are like the the uh the tabs that we have that they're supposed to fill out throughout their entire week."   [58:25]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Management Tracker Tabs"   NÚMEROS:  10+ categorias de tracking   VISUAL:  Spreadsheet com múltiplas abas de rastreamento   [58:30]  "We have our call review log. That's where they have the actual links to the recordings of the call reviews. We have our rep development log, which is the main themes and trends they're consistently seeing that they want us to work on with the team. And that's like their action or training plan."   [58:52]  "We have our lead insights, which are all insights that we see about leads. Is lead quality increasing or decreasing? I'm going to come back to that word lead quality in a second."   [59:08]  "We have an objection matrix, which means what are all the objections we're hearing on calls and what is the rebuttal that we're going to train the team on, right?"   [59:22]  "A pet peeve of mine is when a sales director tells me what the objection is. I don't give a fuck what the objection is. I want to know how you're going to overcome it and how you're training the team on it. So, they have to build out that matrix"   [59:38]  "FAQ matrix, so we could see what people are curious about and asking, competitor log, pitch design insights, offer design insights, one-on- ones, and then account growth ideas."   [59:55]  "That's just like where they, you know, we we found we needed organization because we have always have all these ideas on things that we could do to improve and they never live anywhere. So that we just threw that on the same management tracker"   [1:00:10]  "and so we are constantly reviewing this and every Friday uh Ry guy he manages all the uh I don't know if you guys uh know him I've talked about him before. Uh he uh he manages the sales director. So he does uh these performance reviews with the directors and they go through this entire thing and all their activity KPIs."   [1:00:35]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Lead Quality - Remove From Vocabulary"   NÚMEROS:  Não especificados   VISUAL:  Aviso sobre não aceitar "lead quality" como resposta   [1:00:40]  "Now notice on here it doesn't say anything about lead quality and I'm going to talk about this at the end of the presentation. I literally if somebody tells me that the issue is lead quality I will hang up the freaking Zoom call."   [1:00:55]  "Not literally. I'm not like a dick when I'm managing people. But you have to tell your sales team to remove the words lead quality from their vocabulary cuz it's so useless. It means nothing. It means absolutely nothing. It's completely subjective. Most of the time they're wrong. It doesn't matter."   [1:01:15]  "So if somebody says lead quality, I do my best to not throw something at them. And then I say, 'All right, what was wrong with the quality of the leads?' Right? So you have to really focus on specificity. Never take lead quality as an example. And they better always have data and documentation to support what they're saying is wrong with the leads."   [1:01:40]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Diagnosing Performance - The Validation Sequence"   NÚMEROS:  4 níveis de validação   VISUAL:  Framework sequencial de diagnóstico de performance   [1:01:45]  "All right. Diagnosing performance. Now, the next thing I want to say about call analysis is that most people, the reality is they don't know what to solve when they're listening to calls."   [1:02:00]  "And um you know, when you're scaling a business, you have to solve things in the right sequence. You could do all the right things at the wrong time and it won't work. So, that's a big part with diagnosing performance. And I teach something called the validation sequence."   [1:02:20]  "I think I actually talked about this really briefly last time I spoke at this mastermind. And so there's four levels to a validation sequence, right? I'm going to read this word for word."   [1:02:35]  "When we're listening to sales calls, we have a very structured process on how we're validating and making sure that things are improving as fast as they can. We call this our validation sequence, which is the order in which we validate and prove that each variable of a sales call is working and fully optimized."   [1:03:00]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Validation Sequence Order"   NÚMEROS:  1. Lead Quality, 2. Rep Performance, 3. Process/Pitch, 4. Offer   VISUAL:  Framework de quatro níveis hierárquicos   [1:03:05]  "This is the order we validate in. Number one is lead quality. Now again, we're not saying, 'Oh, it's bad lead quality.' Okay, I'm just saying we are measuring and evaluating the quality of the leads with a high level of specificity."   [1:03:25]  "Here's why. If you don't have the right leads on the phone, you can't really evaluate anything else, right? So, a big part of this, which to not get too long-winded here, there's a lot of stuff I cut out, but we go into lead scoring pretty heavily here and segmentation because you need to know that you're talking to the right people on the phones first before you can evaluate sales performance."   [1:03:52]  "Otherwise, you could have a false negative, right? because you're because what happens is you're saying, 'Well, is it the leads? Is it the sales rep?' Let's remove the subjectivity. Okay, it's not the leads. We know it's not the leads. We're analyzing the leads. We have a good level of consistency."   [1:04:15]  "Number two, if you have the right person on the phone, right? They're financially capable. They're the right fit for your offer. Then we look at rep performance. Is this sales rep doing what they're supposed to be doing?"   [1:04:32]  "So, here's a good example of when they would not be if they're not following the the talk track. Remember, technical compliance. if they're not doing the big three, if they're creating objections."   [1:04:48]  "So, number two is we value uh validate rep performance. That's where most people get lost. And I'm going to tell you this, if you have generally good lead quality, you're talking to the right leads, you have a good sales team, you're watching calls, you are visibly seeing that they are doing what they're supposed to be doing, and you're not hitting the close rates or the uh conversions that you want. It's the process that's wrong. It's the pitch that's wrong. It's the talk track that's wrong."   [1:05:22]  "And the craziest thing and and I and I really want some people to hear this here because what happens is they just start firing everybody because they don't know what's wrong. They freak out. I'm not hitting the row as I want. I'm not profitable. Let's fire the sales team because they're not improving. Right?"   [1:05:42]  "I'm not defending sales reps. Sometimes they need to be fired. But what I'm saying is you have to know when to fire them. Because if they're doing everything well, and I'm not even saying like a godle talent. I'm saying like generally speaking, they're doing a pretty good job and you have 5% close rates, then you have to start looking at the talk track and the pitch design."   [1:06:08]  "And you have to go through multiple different iterations until something lands. If you truly believe that you have the right leads, decent sales talent, and you've cycled through a lot of different pitches and talk tracks, you have to readjust your offer, right?"   [1:06:28]  "It's the offer itself that's not irresistible. Like, and and this doesn't happen often. It doesn't happen often to us at all, especially because we like to sell things that have validation already. But like if you guys really have those few things down and and it's like so freaking hard to be profitable as a company, dude, your offer isn't that good."   [1:06:52]  "You know, it shouldn't be that freaking hard to hit, you know, a 4x rorowaz or whatever it is to hit your guys' numbers. So you have to address the offer. Does that give a lot of clarity?" "All right."   [1:07:10]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "AI Enabled Call Analysis"   NÚMEROS:  Não especificados   VISUAL:  Screenshot de sistema de AI com múltiplos scorecards   [1:07:15]  "In it AI enabled call analysis. This isn't something I'm necessarily teaching. I guess this is something that you would label as a flex, but we are using AI for all of this now, and it's pretty freaking badass."   [1:07:32]  "I It's It's been a long time since I I've been this excited about something that we're doing in our business. That's just a random screenshot of the things that our AI track our AI tracks."   [1:07:48]  "It took us a lot of months to to build this custom configuration and the way that we have it, but our AI call analysis, these are the things that it looks for. It does a qualitative sales scorecard. So it it grades every scorecard."   [1:08:08]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "AI Tracking Categories"   NÚMEROS:  4 scorecards principais   VISUAL:  Lista de categorias: Qualitative, FTC Compliance, Technical Compliance, Product Knowledge   [1:08:12]  "Here's why. You will never be in a situation, never none no one here, including myself, will ever be in a situation where you can have a sales director, no matter how many of them you hire, that will listen to every single sales call that is had because the economics would not make any sense for the business, right?"   [1:08:35]  "So So what what that literally means is you're always managing a sales team through sample sizes, right? which is helpful, but there's a lot that you never get to. And sometimes, unfortunately, the way that it works out, that could be where most of the answers are."   [1:08:55]  "So, I wanted to say, and I challenged my team, my operations team. I said, 'How do we grade every single call?' And so, here's what we do now. It grades every single call from a qualitative perspective."   [1:09:12]  "So, we have a ranking system that we trained from our entire training center, like years of training, years of call reviews, and years of like grading calls. We trained it on that process and now it does it automatically. So every single call gets a score."   [1:09:32]  "Then we have an FTC compliance scorecard that's been completely trained. So it will give a score on FTC compliance. Then we do technical compliance. Not how good the call is, but are they doing the things that they're doing. That one is not a metric. That one is a yes or no. Did they do this? Did they do that? Yes or no?"   [1:09:55]  "And it and it rank and it grades all of it in that way. Then we have a product knowledge scorecard and we automate all the stuff on the tracker."   [1:10:08]  "So check this. Check out how crazy this is. Remember this tracker? We have the lead insights, objection matrix, FAQs, competitors. We trained it where it does all that automatically."   [1:10:25]  "And we have like crazy statistics where it'll be like out of the last 50 calls, 27 of your calls had, you know, challenges with financial capability. So the entire goal is to turn the qualitative insights into quantitative."   [1:10:45]  "And we've been able to do that as of the last few months, which is really, really last few months, which is really exciting." [1:16:55]  "But then when we look underneath, you know, and we get underneath the hood, it's like, 'Yeah, but they had the lowest show rate. They had horrible follow-up. They had a lower average sales price because there's multiple offers. And they're doing a bunch of fuck payment plans. So, the collected rate is so low.' [1:17:22]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Keystone Metrics"   NÚMEROS:  GDPC (Gross Dollars Per Call), CDPC (Collected Dollars Per Call)   VISUAL:  Dashboard mostrando métricas-chave consolidadas   [1:17:28]  "So, what I like to look at, and I just wanted to uh emphasize this, yes, we look at all of those things and we look at them comparatively to all the different reps, but I really like what are called keystone metrics where these metrics don't lie. They give you the entire equation."   [1:17:48]  "And so, the two that you see on the screen with an example dashboard here is GDPC and CDPC, which is I know with a lot of letters, it stands for gross dollars per booked call and collected dollars per booked call."   [1:18:08]  "And Jeremy and I, we did a like a podcast type thing a couple nights ago and uh and we were talking about this concept. And the reason why is this. Now, if we now, okay, as somebody who takes ownership of sales, I always want to improve sales. Always."   [1:18:30]  "There's nobody that has a bigger incentive to improve sales than myself. So, I'm not defensive on the topic. I just have a point to make. [1:18:52]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "ROAS Example Calculation"   NÚMEROS:  $50 cost per call, $400 collected = 8x ROAS, 10% vs 20% close rate   VISUAL:  Exemplo matemático de ROAS por rep   [1:18:58]  "And here's why. And it's really interesting when I break this down. So a lot of people are marketers here and you always talk in terms of rorowaz, right? You talk in terms of rorowaz. Well, think about this for a second."   [1:19:15]  "Close rate, show rate, all that fuck It doesn't tell you everything you need to know. the Keystone metric does because if I am if I have a a sales team and by the way, every single week I do a scoreboard review with all of our sales directors on every single brand and it takes me minutes to look at the scoreboard."   [1:19:38]  "I know exactly what to look at and I know if they're doing their job or not. And what I'll ask Jeremy is I'll say, 'Hey Jeremy, what's the cost per call in this account?' He'll be like, 'It's a hundred bucks.' I'll be like, 'Perfect. Okay, I look at that, look at the close rate. Perfect. Move on.' Don't even care. I move on."   [1:20:00]  "And what happens is even our sales directors, I have to bash this into their brains. Let's say as a hypothetical example, you have sales reps that are closing at 20%. Some of our brand deals have really low cost per calls or maybe they have a strategy like an Instagram shoutout strategy, right?"   [1:20:22]  "Like with Jeremy Moser on a quick shout out like dude, we have an account that literally is getting like $7 cost per calls. It was crazy. And if you guys have ever heard me talk about Jeremy Haynes, he has historically notoriously always had the lowest cost per call on any deal we've done."   [1:20:45]  "I I I seriously and like he said, we don't pay each other referrals. There's just reciprocity because we do well together. Every single time he has come into a deal, the cost per call has lowered drastically."   [1:21:02]  "And so here's what's interesting. Again, hypothetical, sales reps are at 20%. Let's say you got a couple at 10%. What's your first thought? I'll say it for you. Fire them. That's what everybody says."   [1:21:20]  "And I had somebody I had a client tell me that why the you know freaking out on me, right? this industry is weird like that. Like there's a lot of clients that just don't like I don't know, they talk weird. And so I was just getting like chewed out by a client and I'm like hearing this person out."   [1:21:42]  "I was like, 'All right, so you want me to fire this person?' Let me tell you something. For whatever reason, at the time this cost per call was $50. We had sales reps at 20% close rate. One of them was at 10%. Or there's a couple, I think, but their collected dollar per gross call was $400."   [1:22:08]  "Okay, ROAZ on an individual rep level, they're collecting $400 every time there's a call that they paid 50 bucks for, right? What's the basic math on that?" "What do you say? It's a It's like a 8x rorowaz, right? On an individual rep basis, it's a 8x rorowaz."   [1:22:32]  "Am I going to have my team do everything they can to get the 10 to the 20%. Yeah, obviously I'm not a fuck but I'm not going to fire this rep when it's still an 8x rorowaz on that specific account because of the economics at hand."   [1:22:52]  "What I will absolutely do is put them on a success implementation plan, which is like a PIP, and work with them and and have the directors focus on the underdeveloping reps and maybe even hire a new rep and as soon as they outperform them, I'll replace them."   [1:23:12]  "But I'm not going to fire them because of the amount of money it would cost. That's why I look at these keystone metrics. All the other vanity stuff doesn't really matter to me. I just want to know for every dollar you pay for a call, how many are we getting back? And I look at it on an individual rep basis. And that's how we make the decision."   [1:23:35]  "Now, pro tip here. The only time everything I just said doesn't apply is if your calendars are not at max capacity. All right? Meaning you have calls that you could still book. Why? Well, because now opportunity cost obviously plays plays into that."   [1:23:58]  "I can take the the the clo the calls that are getting me back 400 bucks. I could give them to the other closer and get me back 800 bucks. So now I will fire them and just max out the capacity on the top closes. But if all else is equal, this is the logic that we take."   [1:24:20]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Quantitative vs Qualitative"   NÚMEROS:  Não especificados   VISUAL:  Comparação entre o que números mostram vs por que eles são assim   [1:24:25]  "All right. So the quantitative the quantitative tells you what the qualitative tells you why. I never ask my sales directors what the performance is on the team. I don't need them to tell me. We have a dashboard. I just showed it to you."   [1:24:45]  "Even before the dashboards, we've had all spreadsheets. Like it's a number. I just look at it. So the only value that they could give me in that situation is why the numbers are what they are. That's the most important. I can see what it says. Why is it what it is?"   [1:25:08]  "All right. A great sales manager should be able to say exactly what's going on with each rep with extreme spe uh specificity. Again, another pet peeve of mine and when I always challenge our team to level up, I always focus on this."   [1:25:28]  "Like, I don't care what the lead quality is. I don't care. You know, when you ask, you're looking at a scoreboard, right? Close rates are lower. You're like, 'Dude, what's going on?' Imagine the answer. It's like, what kind of answer is that?"   [1:26:08]  "What do you do with that information? You can't act in generics, in conceptual, in abstract in sales management. I need a high level of specificity, you know, cuz I'll give a high level of specificity to the sales director what they're doing wrong."   [1:26:28]  "And if they're not handling the performance, now I have to handle the performance, which means they're going to get fired. That's how it goes. They take ownership of the rep KPIs. We take uh well Ry guy takes ownership of their KPIs but then I ultimately have to take responsibility for everybody's KPIs right"   [1:26:50]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Conditional Logic Based Performance Management"   NÚMEROS:  Multiple if/then scenarios   VISUAL:  Matriz de decisão condicional para gestão de performance   [1:26:55]  "so what we do is called conditional logic based performance management right you guys all know conditional logic if X then why we do that for performance why because at at my core I'm an operator right it's all about sales operations not sales"   [1:27:15]  "I'm an amazing salesperson put me on the phone I will run it up I love sales I still get in the trenches on calls I'm going to show you guys that in a second or I'll talk about that in a second, but I'm an operator at heart and I'm always trying to figure out like why is this one thing not working and I want to like plan it out surgically."   [1:27:38]  "So here's the thing. Anytime you can standardize and remove subjectivity, you do it period. Yes, you want to scale decision-m, right? I need a management team that can critically think, but I'm not going to have them spend their brain power critical thinking about things that can just be completely standardized, right?"   [1:28:00]  "performance management is one of them. And if you guys don't have like as business owners or sales managers, whatever you are, like if you don't have if your team doesn't have that, how can you hold them responsible when your sales team isn't performing? You've never set the bar, right?"   [1:28:22]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Conditional Logic Example - Ramping Phase"   NÚMEROS:  Days 8-38, 2-week sales cycle, 14 days   VISUAL:  Exemplo detalhado de lógica condicional para fase de ramping   [1:28:28]  "So here's an example. This is one slide of a mini page deck of our conditional logic based performance management based on the phase that they're at in certain situations or certain uh criteria, I guess you'd call it."   [1:28:45]  "Again, it says if X then why? It's very straightforward. So, let's read it. If they're ramping, so they're days 8 through 38. Why? They were onboarding the first week and then they're building a pipeline. It's a twoe sales cycle and then they need everything to come to maturity. So, 8 to 38, let's call it in the ramping phase."   [1:29:12]  "If the KPIs are not met, but the calls sound really good, trending in the right direction, and pipeline is building, when you look at the forecasted volume, what do you do? Continue ramping. Very simple."   [1:29:30]  "If the KPIs are not met, right? Let me just say that again. By the way, that was an example for the KPIs not met, right? The KPIs are not met, but all of these things are true. What do you do? Continue ramping, right?"   [1:29:48]  "If the KPIs are not met and the calls don't sound good, they don't have technical compliance, the pipeline is not increasing. When you look at the forecasted volume, address in a one-on-one additional support and let them know what we need that we need to see significant changes over the next two weeks."   [1:30:12]  "And in parenthesis, I put 14 days because everything that we do is for a reason. And if you put two weeks and all of a sudden someone's like, well, was it like business days or like calendar days? And then it's like all these other questions happen from it, right? It's like very simple. Two weeks, 14 days, this is what you do, right?"   [1:30:35]  "And so we have this for every single level of performance. And it it actually gets really complicated. And anytime we have a like a situation that we haven't occurred or that hasn't occurred or it's like nuance for a specific brand, we add it to this."   [1:30:55]  "I'll give you guys an example of one. Pull the dashboard up, bring the matrix up, what does it say to do? We just look one rep at a time every single week."   [1:31:18]  "All right, so here's an example. We had a brand and we had some reps that were performing a lot worse than other reps on daily lead flow, but then they had an event like Jeremy was talking about. They do webinars every single month and those reps that were underperforming on daily lead flow were overperforming on the webinar."   [1:31:42]  "So, it's like my management seems like, what do we do, Josh? Like, do we fire them? Cuz like they're like opposites, you know? So, a couple things that we did. Number one, we started kind of like equalizing the entire decision where we looked at uh those Keystone metrics as a whole."   [1:32:05]  "Well, as a whole, if you aggregate all the calls, who's given me back more money for for every call they take than the dollars that we're paying for it. So, that's one way we equalized the situation."   [1:32:22]  "The second thing is we put qualifiers where it was like, you have to hit uh we we had qualifiers for both. You have to hit at least X here and X here to qualify for X commission. So then at the end of the day, like people are in sales because they want to make money."   [1:32:42]  "So usually you have to kind of mess with the money to make some of these things work. That's just an example of how you have to like there's no subjectivity in there. And then the coolest thing about that is I write it one time, I put it in the deck, I give it to the team, I never have to make that decision for them again because they just look at it every single time. Right?"   [1:33:08]  "All right. By the way, guys, like this is what it looks like at scale, right? Like a like so much of these systems I've learned. Yeah. One quick second, Omar."   [1:33:22]  "Uh so many of these systems I've learned because like it's not that hard with one sales manager. Well, even when you hire one sales manager, if it's not you now, they need to make the decisions that you would make if you're not there."   [1:33:38]  "But like you could you could figure it out, but what happens when you have two? What happens when you have three? What happens when you have six? What happens when you have 10? Right? We had to consistently figure out like how does everybody make the same decision the same way? Yeah. Omar,"   [1:33:58]  "I had a question. So, when you're measuring closing rate, are you measuring schedule to close or help to close?"   [1:34:08]  "Well, to be fair, it doesn't really matter because as long as you're actually comparing the same things and the same metrics and you know what you're looking for and you know what your baselines are. Um, but we look at both."   [1:34:25]  "The thing that most people don't know, but and you don't know this by way of asking that question, it's going to tell the same story as long as you understand. Here's what I mean. If you have a 15% live call close rate, it's a 50% close and that's a seven and a half booked close rate. Does that make sense?"   [1:34:48]  "So, I don't really need to know I only need to know one or the other and then I just need to know the show rate and I know what that means anyways. So, if you want a 20% close rate and you have the 50% show rate, then I already know that's a 10% booked call close rate. So, it doesn't matter that much as long as you know what you're looking for. That make sense?" "Cool. Get an iPad."   [1:35:15]  "Great question." "Here, hand that to him."   [1:35:20]  "All right, so going back off of that, uh, do better is not a plan."   [1:35:28]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Do Better Is Not A Plan - Hyperspecific Improvement Plans"   NÚMEROS:  Não especificados   VISUAL:  Template de Success Implementation Plan   [1:35:32]  "All right, I only have a few more slides here, guys. We'll take a break. I know this stuff is long. Uh, do better is not a plan. So, just going off of that performance management matrix, then what you have to do, hyperspecific improvement plans."   [1:35:52]  "Hyper specific like it's got to be so spec. Sometimes it's like, listen, you have to do this one thing over the next week. that is the only thing you'll be judged on. We're going to listen to your calls. But if I hear it one freaking time that you don't do this, dude, the next conversation is not going to be fun, right?"   [1:36:15]  "Sometimes it has to be that detailed because sales reps can't focus on that many things at a time when they're like trying to think of everything else on the sales call. So, it's very similar to what we were saying about call analysis when we're breaking the feedback down to phases."   [1:36:35]  "So, that's a screenshot. That's like a template of our success implementation plan. It's kind of like a performance improvement plan, but it's a success implementation plan."   [1:36:50]  "I don't have these different terms to be cute. It's just because like I was saying, I'm really bought in on leadershipbased uh or excuse me, influence-based leadership. And like I I want every all the branding internally to be like, 'Dude, this is here. This you're not in trouble, bro. I'm going to show you how you can make more money, right?' Like it's all framed in that way."   [1:37:15]  "But hey, at the same time, I'm only going to look out for your money to the extent that you'll look out for mine. I want to make more money through you. Do you want to make more money? Because if you don't want to make the same amount of money as I want to make, the economics of our of our relationship don't make sense anymore and we have to part ways."   [1:37:38]  "It's a pretty simple conversation. We're both just trying to make money, right? That's how we treat the sales reps."   [1:37:48]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "SIP Components"   NÚMEROS:  4 seções principais   VISUAL:  Estrutura do Success Implementation Plan: Performance Summary, Root Cause Analysis, Clear Expectations, Evaluation & Action Plan   [1:37:52]  "So, what we do in this plan is we give a performance summary. We do root cause analysis because most of the time they can't see what they're actually doing wrong. So, we have to tell them. We give very clear expectations for improvement. And then we give an evaluation and an action plan."   [1:38:15]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Evaluation of Implementation"   NÚMEROS:  24-48 hours follow-up window   VISUAL:  Framework de probabilidade de mudança de resultados   [1:38:20]  "Here's what you need to know. evaluation of implementation. All right. Again, a really big mistake that I've seen and uh and guys, I said it at the beginning of the the uh presentation, like I know this fuck cuz I've been in the trenches doing it for a very long time."   [1:38:42]  "I have never once said we're perfect and we don't make mistakes. I said at the very beginning, we had the same problems as all of you. So, I have to get with our management team a lot of the times and remind them of these things."   [1:39:00]  "Be like, listen, you're telling the rep what to do. You're giving them these improvement plans. What are you doing to make sure that they're actually successful in them? Because what I refuse to do, and I actually had to do this last week, I had to do this with the sales director last week."   [1:39:22]  "I get on the scoreboard review call. I say, 'All right, show me your dashboard.' They show me the numbers. It's like, 'All right, why are the numbers what they are?' They start saying the same exact thing they said last week and then and the week before."   [1:39:42]  "I said, 'All right, the meeting's over. I'm going to hang up now. Send me this specific detail on why the numbers are are why they are.' And I hung up the call. I legitimately did because I'm not going to do Groundhog's Day."   [1:40:00]  "I don't want to hear the same fuck over and over and over again when it gives me no value, when it gives me no insight, when I know that's not going to fix the problem. So, it's all about the evaluation of implementation."   [1:40:18]  "Here's what you do when you give really specific feedback to reps with, like I said, with lagging indicators. You can't just force revenue through a sales cycle, but it's also not an option to just wait for nothing to change."   [1:40:38]  "So, basically, what you're doing, guys, it's it's it's really math is you're playing a game of probabilities, right? It's all probability. What is the probability that the results will change? And your confidence level for the profitability is based on the evidence that you find on the calls. It's that simple."   [1:41:02]  "So listen for the speed of the implementation on calls. You have to see proof that there's a high probability of a different future outcome. Does that make sense?"   [1:41:18]  "I just like to explain it in a in a little bit of a different way than most people do because if you like I said, take that one example. I'll give you guys an example and I talk about this again. I think in the next presentation, one really, really, really big thing that you should all write down if you're not doing this, just so you don't forget it"   [1:41:42]  "is if your reps have to set up a follow-up call, they need to set the follow-up within 24 to 48 hours. [1:42:02]  "If there are no other times that work within 24 hours, we say, 'What do they have in the other 48 hours?' [1:42:22]  "Like, I'm just asking you because you were telling me a few minutes ago that this is absolutely a priority. You've tried a lot of things. You can't figure it out. [1:54:30]  "so they could maximize efficiency and minimize revenue leakage. When you really start scaling a business, you start to get to a point where you decide what problems you want to solve."   [1:54:48]  "And I think I'll speak for all of us when I say we get really sick of solving some of the same problems over and over again. [1:55:10]  "So finally I got to the point where I was like, you know what? I don't even give a fuck I'm gonna change the problem I'm solving and I'm gonna remove all the critical thinking from the CRM." [2:09:20]  "So the first bucket is going to be P1 which would be like hot new leads. The second buckets and that would be from like contacts one to three. They keep hitting that list until it has three contacts. It goes into the second priority bucket. That will be titled warm leads. That's contacts three to six."   [2:09:42]  "And then the last bucket, I don't know what the name is, but it's the last bucket. And then you would hit them six, seven, eight, and then they would be closed out."   [2:09:52]  "So what we do is we compare that based on the proper setter capacity, based on the amount of time that they've been in that bucket, and you have a deal rotting feature. So, if you're supposed to hit a whole bucket of leads three times each contact in three days, you have a deal rotting thing where it will show that lead turns red if it's been in that bucket longer than 3 days without three contacts.
